tldr: Swap Bugsnag's error monitoring for Airbrake's. Both are mature SaaS error trackers with per-language SDKs and similar concepts, so the migration is mostly mechanical: re-instrument apps, re-upload sourcemaps/symbols, and rebuild integrations.
why_switch: Escape Bugsnag's commit-based event tiers locked for the billing cycle
Airbrake's freemium with metered overages can fit smaller teams better
Different roadmap, alerting, and triage UX may suit your workflow
Both expose similar SDK surface, keeping code changes contained
Diversify away from a vendor whose pricing structure penalizes spikes
what_to_export: Project list and per-project API keys
Historical errors with stack traces and grouping data
Release/version records and deploy tracking history
Custom metadata fields and tag schemas
export_method: Use the Bugsnag web UI or its documented Data Access API to pull errors, events, and project metadata. Download uploaded sourcemaps and symbol files so they can be re-uploaded to Airbrake for the same release versions. If a bulk export is not exposed on your plan, paginate through the API and store JSON to object storage. Document custom severity, tags, and notification rules in a spreadsheet — these are easier to recreate than to import. Keep Bugsnag read-only for a retention window so teams can reference closed issues. Note that historical events typically cannot be imported into Airbrake, so the export is archival; new events flow into Airbrake from cutover forward.
import_method: Create an Airbrake account and a project per environment. Replace Bugsnag SDK calls with the matching Airbrake library for each language (e.g., @airbrake/browser, airbrake-ruby, pybrake) using the project ID and key from Airbrake. Re-implement breadcrumbs, user context, and custom metadata using Airbrake's notifier API. Upload sourcemaps, ProGuard mappings, and dSYMs to Airbrake via its CLI or upload endpoint, tied to the release version. Recreate alert rules and connect Slack, PagerDuty, Jira, etc., from scratch in Airbrake's integration settings. Run both Bugsnag and Airbrake SDKs in parallel in staging to compare grouping and noise before cutting over. Verify production by triggering a known error and confirming it appears with the expected stack trace and metadata in Airbrake.
gotchas: Stability scores are Bugsnag-specific; you cannot replicate the exact metric in Airbrake
Grouping algorithms differ — expect different issue counts and noisiness right after cutover
Sourcemap and symbol uploads must match release versions or stack traces stay raw
Airbrake's open-source main repo shows long gaps between commits; verify SDK activity for your specific language before committing
Bugsnag's commit-based pricing means the contract may continue billing until the term ends regardless of usage
Run both SDKs in parallel before cutover to avoid a quiet outage if Airbrake misroutes any errors

email.subject: Notice of contract non-renewal — switching error monitoring vendor
email.body:
Hi team,

We are writing to give notice that we will not be renewing our Bugsnag subscription at the end of the current term. Within the next 14 days we plan to complete migration to Airbrake.

The primary driver is the commit-based event tier model, which locks our spend for the billing cycle and does not match how our error volume actually moves. We need a structure that scales down as well as up.

To wrap up cleanly we would like to:

- Export all historical errors, sourcemaps, and symbol files from our projects.
- Confirm the final invoice, contract end date, and any retention or off-boarding requirements.
- Keep the account in read-only mode for an agreed retention window.

Please share your off-boarding checklist and the contact for the data export.

Thanks for the service over the past period.

Your customer
